segments.s202-g65: سر بدره‌ها را is the direct object phrase (سر + plural poetic epithet بدره‌ها) marked by the object marker را. گشادن is the older/poetic infinitive ‘to open/reveal’ (classical form instead of modern گشودن). گرفت here follows that infinitive and functions idiomatically with the infinitive to mean 'began to ___' (the literal verb گرفت normally means 'took/seized' but is used as an auxiliary meaning 'began to' in such compounds). Word order places the object before the verb, as is normal when را marks the object.
segments.s202-g66: نهاده is a perfect/past participle of نهادن 'placed/put' and introduces a participial clause ('having placed' / 'having set'). همه رای is the object phrase 'all (everyone's) counsel/opinions' (رای here = counsel/advice). دادن گرفت is the same infinitive + گرفت construction: دادن (to give) plus گرفت = 'began to give' or 'took to giving' — i.e. he started issuing/giving (orders, advice, decisions). The clause is sequential/causal: after/once he had set (نهاده) the counsel, he began to give.
segments.s202-g67: کشیدند is a simple past plural of کشیدن; in epic contexts it commonly means 'set out / drew up / marched' (literal senses include 'to pull/draw'). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. لشکر is the direct noun 'army/host'. بیاراستند is the past plural (poetic form with prefix bi-) meaning 'they adorned/arrayed/trimmed' — here 'they arrayed the army.'
segments.s202-g68: ز هر چیز is a prepositional phrase 'with respect to / from every thing' (ز = from/of; together here functioning as 'as for everything' or 'in every respect'). لختی is an adverb meaning 'for a short while' (classical usage) that modifies بپیراستند. بپیراستند is the past plural of (بـ)پیراستن 'to arrange/put in order/prepare' (prefix ب- + verb) meaning 'they arranged/put in order' — so the clause says they, in a short time, set everything in order.
segments.s202-g25: This is a temporal clause introduced by چو 'when'; ز پیش is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'from ahead' (ز + پیش). طلایه سواری is a compound noun 'vanguard of horsemen' (طلایه modifying سواری). گرد here is literal 'dust' raised by the approaching riders and combines with چو ... بیامد to mean 'when (they) came, dust (was raised)'.
segments.s202-g26: بیامد is the past verb 'came' closing the temporal clause. The following main clause has an implicit subject (he); سخنها همه modifies یاد کرد: سخنها 'the words/speeches', همه 'all', and یاد کرد is the compound 'یاد کردن' with کرد as the past auxiliary meaning 'recalled/mentioned'.
segments.s202-g27: که introduces the clause (here roughly 'then' / 'that' linking the action). رهام گودرز are two proper names placed in apposition (no conjunction needed). زان رزمگاه is an archaic prepositional phrase: زان = 'from that', رزمگاه = 'battlefield'.
segments.s202-g28: بیامد is the past verb 'came'. سوی is the preposition 'toward', and پهلوان سپاه is a genitival phrase 'the champion of the army' (pahlewan = champion, followed by سپاه 'host/army'). The subject is omitted again and understood from context.
segments.s202-g29: بفرمود is an honorific past verb 'he ordered/commanded'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that/in order that' and takes the subjunctive/imperative form آورند 'they bring'. پیش اوی = 'before him/in his presence' where اوی is the third‑person pronoun referring back to the addressee (object of پیش).
segments.s202-g30: This continues the result clause: two coordinated adjectives/given descriptions گشاده‌دل 'open‑hearted/generous' و 'and' تازه‌روی 'fresh‑faced/newcomer' describe the person brought. آورند repeats the verb 'they bring' (same subjunctive/purpose sense from the previous clause).
segments.s202-g31: سراینده رهام is a genitival phrase 'the poet/singer of Roham' (سراینده + name). شد is the past copular/verb here meaning roughly 'came/appeared' (classical usage can render 'came to be present'), and پیش اوی places him 'before him/in his presence' with اوی the 3rd‑person pronoun.
segments.s202-g32: بترس is an imperative 'be afraid' or idiomatically 'beware'. از marks the object/cause 'of/from', and نهان بداندیش اوی is a noun phrase 'his hidden ill‑thought/secret ill‑will' where بداندیش is 'bad‑minded/ill‑thinking' and اوی is the possessive 3rd‑person referring back to the person warned about.
segments.s202-g49: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. ایدونک is a small temporal/colloquial particle meaning 'at this time / for now' (poetic). یک ماه is the duration 'one month' with خواهی as a 2nd-person present/future meaning 'you want / you wish' followed by the noun درنگ 'delay' — together 'if you want a month’s delay'.
segments.s202-g50: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. لشکر is the source 'army/host'. نیاید is negative present/subjunctive 'does not come / will not come'. سواری means 'riders / mounted troops'. بجنگ here denotes motion toward fighting ('to the fight' / 'to battle'). The whole clause: 'no mounted troops will come from the army to the battle.'
segments.s202-g51: وگر = 'but if / if'. جنگ جویی is a compound where جنگ (battle) is followed by the deverbal/relational -جویی 'seeking/pursuit', i.e. 'if you seek battle'. منم is emphatic 'I (myself) / I am', and برکنار is adverbial 'aside / put aside' — 'if you seek battle, put me aside' (i.e. leave me out).
segments.s202-g52: This clause gives commands. بیارای is an imperative/hortative form meaning 'array / make ready' (archaic/poetic imperative of 'آراستن'). و = 'and'. برکش is another imperative 'raise/draw up' (here said of the battle line). صف کارزار = 'the line/formation of battle' (صف 'line', کارزار 'battle').
segments.s202-g53: چو functions as 'when/if'. یک مه = 'for one month' (duration). بدین is 'with/by this' (به + این in older spelling), followed by آرزو 'hope/desire'. بشمرید is a jussive/imperative or 2pl present meaning 'consider / count as' (classical subjunctive/optative form) — 'if/when you count this as your hope for a month.'
segments.s202-g54: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that / (namely) that'. از = 'from / beyond' (marking the border as what is to be crossed). مرز توران‌زمین = 'the frontier/border of the land of Turan'. بگذرید is 2nd-person plural present/subjunctive 'you pass / you will pass' (narrative optative sense: 'that you will cross').
segments.s202-g55: برانید is an imperative plural (from راندن) 'drive/lead'. لشکر is the direct object 'the army'. سوی = 'toward'. مرز خویش ش is the possessive phrase 'your own frontier' (خوی + ش where ش = his/her/its — in classical Persian this yields 'their/your own'). The whole: 'lead the army toward your frontier.'
segments.s202-g56: ببینید is an imperative/2nd-plural 'see / examine'. یکسر here is an adverb 'all at once / immediately'. همه ارز خویش = 'all your wealth/treasure' (همه 'all', ارز 'wealth/money', خوی + ش again 'your own'). So: 'immediately inspect all your wealth.'
segments.s202-g1: فریبرز is the subject (proper name). بنهاد is an archaic/past-form of نهادن 'to place; to set' used here in narration (past). The word order is slightly inverted compared with modern Persian (verb close to subject), and بر سر کلاه literally marks the location 'upon (the) head (a) hat' — i.e. 'placed a hat on (his) head' (بر = on/upon, سر = head, کلاه = hat).
segments.s202-g2: که introduces a subordinate clause explaining/reasoning 'for/that'. هم ... هم is the correlative 'both ... and'. پهلوان is a predicate noun 'a champion/hero' and بود is the past copula 'was'. پور شاه is a compound meaning 'son/descendant of the king' (پور = son, شاه = king).
segments.s202-g3: ازان پس is a temporal/discourse phrase 'after that/thereupon'. بفرمود is the honorific past of فرمودن 'he commanded/said', used here as 'he ordered'. رهام را uses the object marker را attached to the personal name to show Roham as the direct object/recipient of the order.
segments.s202-g4: که introduces the content of the order ('that' / purpose). پیدا کند uses پیدا + کند (present/subjunctive) — after a verb of ordering this expresses what he should do: 'that he find'. با here functions as 'with' or a linking preposition before the name phrase; گهر نام را is the object (گهر نام = the name 'Gohar' or 'Gohar-nam'), with را marking it definite/accusative. Word order is somewhat poetic (object flagged by را at end).
segments.s202-g5: بدو is the emphatic/inclitic form 'to him' (i.e. 'he said to him'). گفت here is the narrative verb 'said' (the listed senses are conversational but the function is 'he said'). رو پیش is an instruction: رو (toward/go) + پیش 'in the presence of' — together 'go before/in front of'. پیران is the noun 'elders' and خرام is a verbal/adjectival form meaning 'to stride/prance' (describing how he should go).
segments.s202-g6: ز من is 'from me' (ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from'). نزد آن پهلوان = 'to/into the presence of that champion' (نزد = in the presence of; آن = that; پهلوان = champion). بر پیام literally 'with a message' or 'bearing a message' — بر here means 'toward/for' in a compound sense, and پیام is 'message'; thus the whole phrase means 'carry a message from me to that champion.'
segments.s202-g7: بگویش is the imperative/optative 'tell him' (بگو + enclitic ش for 'to him'). که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. کردار گردان سپهر is a poetic noun phrase: کردار 'deeds/actions', گردان 'turning/revolving' (qualifying کردار), and سپهر 'the sky/heavens' (here fate/cosmos); together roughly 'the revolving deeds of heaven' or 'the turns of heaven's course'.
segments.s202-g8: همیشه چنین بود is a past narrative: همیشه 'always', چنین 'thus/so', بود 'was' — 'it has always been thus' (past used to state a general truth). پر درد و مهر = 'full of درد (pain/sorrow) and مهر (love/affection)'; پر is a prepositional/adjectival 'full (of)', and درد here is emotional sorrow (not only physical). مهر is 'love/affection' in this context.
segments.s202-g33: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. The clause has the verb دید (past 'saw') after the subject پیران (Piran) — a common poetic word order — and ورا is the old dative/accusative 'to him'. بنواختش is a past tense form with the b- prefix plus نواخت and the pronominal clitic -ش 'him'; in classical usage it means 'addressed/struck (at) him' (poetic/archaic).
segments.s202-g34: بپرسید is a past-tense verb 'he asked' (b- prefix + پرسید). The conjunction و links the next action. بر تخت is a prepositional phrase 'on the throne' placed before the verb for emphasis, and بنشاختش is a past verb meaning 'he seated/placed him', with the enclitic -ش referring back to 'him'. This is a terse, poetic sequence of actions.
segments.s202-g35: برآورد is a past verb 'brought out / produced' (poetic b‑prefix on آردن-type root) and appears before its subject (رهام) in this inverted, literary order. راز is the direct object 'secret' and از نهفت 'from concealment' is a prepositional phrase explaining the source; نهفت is an adjective/noun 'hidden/that which is hidden'.
segments.s202-g36: پیام فریبرز is a noun phrase 'the message of Faryburz' (possession by juxtaposition typical in poetry). با او b.gفت combines با 'to/with' marking the indirect object 'to him' and بگفت is the past 'he said / he told' (b- prefix + گفت). Together it means 'he told the message to him.'
segments.s202-g37: چنین 'thus' introduces speech. گفت here functions as the verb 'said' (classical word order places the verb before the subject), پیران is the subject 'Piran', and برهام گرد is an older poetic expression meaning 'turned toward Roham' (بر = toward/to + رهام = Roham; گرد is the verb 'turned'). The phrase describes Piran's action as he speaks.
segments.s202-g38: که is the subordinating particle 'that' introducing the content of what was said. این جنگ را marks 'this battle' as the definite object (را), خرد here means 'wisdom' or 'reason' and is the standard instrument/criterion in the clause. نتوان is the negative modal 'cannot' and شمرد is 'to count/reckon'; together نتوان شمرد = 'cannot be reckoned/ counted.'
segments.s202-g39: شما را places 'you' as the topical object ('as for you'). بد این پیش‌دستی is a noun phrase 'this bad preemption/initiative' (بد qualifying پیش‌دستی). بجنگ is an infinitival/poetic verbal form meaning 'in battle' or 'to fight' (here describing the sphere of the action). The clause sets up what was (not) observed of 'you'.
segments.s202-g40: ندیدیم is the past plural negative 'we did not see'. با introduces attendant circumstances: طوس رای is 'Tūs's counsel/plan' by juxtaposition (possession without a genitive marker, common in poetry), and و درنگ adds 'and delay/hesitation' — together 'with Tūs's counsel and delay'. The whole finalizes the statement 'we have not seen (you do) such a thing with Tūs's counsel and delay.'
segments.s202-g9: The clause begins with the object phrase 'یکی را' (someone, marked by the definite/object marker را) placed before the verb for emphasis, a common poetic order. 'برآرد' is a third-person singular present/poetic-present form meaning 'raises' or 'brings up'. 'بچرخ بلند' is a compact poetic noun phrase—literally 'the high-turning (wheel)'—acting as the agent or cause (the wheel of fortune); the adjective 'بلند' follows the noun, giving the phrase 'high/lofty'.
segments.s202-g10: Again the object 'یکی را' precedes the verb 'کند' (3rd-singular present 'makes/does'), so syntactically 'it makes one ...'. The sequence of adjectives 'زار و خوار و نژند' are predicative complements describing the object ('wretched, despised, base'), linked by 'و' ('and'). The verb 'کند' governs these complements and yields the meaning 'renders someone X, Y, and Z.'
segments.s202-g11: 'کسی کو' is a nominative/indefinite phrase functioning like 'someone whom' (classical/coarse relative use of کو). 'بلاجست' is an adverb meaning 'at once' or 'immediately' modifying the verbal idea, and 'گرد آن' ('around that / about him') gives a circumstantial phrase; finally 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. The whole clause therefore says there existed someone about whom calamity at once gathered/sought.
segments.s202-g12: This is a simple nominal clause with subject 'شبیخون' (a night-raid) negated by the particle 'نه' and followed by the predicate 'کردار مردان' ('the conduct/way of men/heroes', a genitive noun phrase). 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. Word order (subject — negation — predicate — copula) is characteristic of poetic diction.
segments.s202-g13: The clause 'شبیخون نسازند کنداوران' places the verb 'نسازند' (3rd-person plural present/poetic 'do/make', here negative in context) after the noun for stylistic inversion: the plural agent 'کنداوران' ('those who dig/excavate' in literal sense, here a term for a type of fighter) follows the verb. The literal sense is 'the excavators/sappers do not make night-raids', a succinct poetic statement.
segments.s202-g14: 'کسی کو' is the object phrase 'someone' (classical accusative/relative-like use). 'گراید' is a 3rd-person form meaning 'strikes/turns upon' (here 'strikes' or 'falls upon'), and the instrumental phrase 'بگرز گران' (with the heavy mace) follows, where the prefix 'ب-' marks the instrument 'with/by' and 'گران' is the adjective 'heavy'. Together the clause says that if someone is afflicted, it is by the heavy mace.
segments.s202-g15: 'تو‌گر' is a conditional 'if you' (classical contraction). 'با درنگی' is an instrumental phrase 'with a little delay/hesitation' and 'درنگ' is the noun 'pause/delay' that is being caused. 'آوریم' is first-person plural present/poetic-subjunctive 'we bring' or 'we will cause'. The clause is a conditional offer: if you cause hesitation, we too will produce delay — the repetition is poetic emphasis.
segments.s202-g16: 'گرت' is another conditional 'if (to you)' or 'if you', followed by 'رای' meaning 'plan/counsel'. 'جنگست' is a fused predicate 'it is war' and the repeated noun 'جنگ' ('war') is the object/theme; 'آوریم' (we bring) is the same first-person plural verb as above. The clause reads plainly: 'If your counsel is war, we will bring (i.e., wage) war.'
segments.s202-g57: This clause is an imperative exhortation. وگرنه ("otherwise") opens a conditional/emphatic contrast. بجنگ is an adverbial phrase formed with the preposition-like b- + جنگ meaning “into battle/for fighting.” اندر is the locative preposition “in/into,” and آرید is the archaic 2nd‑person plural present/subjunctive/imperative form of آوردن (“bring, take”); its object is چنگ (“grip, claw”), here a metaphor for weapon or firm hold. Word order places the adverbial/locative elements before the verb, typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s202-g58: مخواهید is a negative imperative/optative addressed to the addressees (“do not desire / do not ask for”). زین پس is the frozen phrase “from this time on” (زین here functions as part of the idiom rather than the literal ‘saddle’), followed by زمان و درنگ “time and delay” as the objects/concepts being rejected. The coordinating و links the two nouns. The clause therefore means “seek no more time or delay henceforth.”
segments.s202-g59: یکی is an indefinite subject (“someone” / “a certain one”). خلعت (“robe of honour”) is the direct object being given. آراست is the past tense 3rd‑person singular of آراستن (“adorned, dressed”), and رهام را is the definite object marked by را — the recipient Roham. The verb follows the subject and precedes the marked object, a word order common in Ferdowsi’s verse.
segments.s202-g60: چنانچون is a comparative/conjunctive phrase “just as / in the way that.” بود is the past copula “was.” درخور functions as a predicative adjective “fitting / appropriate,” and نام را (“his name / reputation,” marked by را) is what the fittingness is measured against. Together the clause reads “just as was fitting to his name.”
segments.s202-g61: بنزد is a prepositional phrase meaning “to the presence of / before.” It governs the following proper name فریبرز (Fariburz). رهام گرد functions as a nearby locative/adverbial phrase “in the (presence/around) Roham” (گرد here carrying the sense “around / near”), placing the action in Roham’s vicinity. The whole phrase sets the goal and circumstance for the following verb.
segments.s202-g62: بیاورد is a perfective/past form meaning “he brought / he produced” and takes نامه (“the letter”) as its direct object (unstressed, placed after the verb in poetry). چنانچون introduces a simile/comparison “just as,” and ببرد (here a past or resultive sense of برد) means “he had carried / he had taken” — so the clause says he brought the letter to Fariburz in Roham’s presence just as he had carried it.
segments.s202-g63: چون here introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning “when / as soon as.” یافت is the past tense “he found,” and روز درنگ is a noun phrase “a day (of) respite / a day free from delay” (درنگ = delay/pause). The clause therefore gives the temporal setting: “When Fariburz found a day without delay…”
segments.s202-g64: بهر سو is an adverbial phrase “to every side / toward all directions” (بهر = toward, سو = side/direction). بیازید is an archaic perfective meaning “he burst forth / sprang” (from یاختن), used here as the main verb. چون is a simile marker “like,” and شیرچنگ (“lion‑claw”) is a metaphor describing his ferocious, grasping onset. Word order places the adverbial before the verb and the simile after it for emphasis, typical of epic diction.
segments.s202-g41: This is a compact classical word order: 'بمرز اندر آمد' = 'to the border (بمرز) into (اندر) came (آمد)'; then 'چو گرگ سترگ' is a simile introduced by چو 'like/as' describing the subject as 'a wolf ...'. The verb آمد is past narrative; چو + noun phrase attaches as a likeness of the coming actor.
segments.s202-g42: همی here is the classical progressive/adverbial marker meaning 'continually/kept on'; کشت is the verbal root meaning 'killed' (past narrative), so 'همی کشت' = 'kept killing'. بی‌باک is an adjectival modifier 'fearless', and خرد و بزرگ lists the victims 'small and great' (small = خرد, بزرگ = great); the conjunction و simply links the two nouns.
segments.s202-g43: چه مایه is an idiomatic classical phrase meaning 'what amount/how many'; it governs two parallel verbal phrases linked by و. بکشت and ببرد are older poetic forms with the prefixed ب-, used here as past narrative verbs ('he killed' and 'he took/carry off'). The structure lists two outcomes (killed, taken) as objects of the opening phrase.
segments.s202-g44: بدو is 'to him/for him' (dative), here marking who benefits; نیک is an evaluative adjective 'good' (he judged it favorable); این مرز is the noun phrase 'this border' (direct object/topic of judgment); یکسان شمرد is a compound predicate: یکسان 'equal' + شمرد (past of شمردن) 'he reckoned/considered'. Together: 'he considered this border equally good to him.'
segments.s202-g45: مکافات این بد is a noun phrase 'the retribution/punishment for this evil' (مکافات = retribution, این بد = 'this bad thing/evil'). کنون is an adverb 'now', and یافتند is the past-tense verb 'they found/received'. The sequence places the temporal adverb before the verb for narrative emphasis.
segments.s202-g46: اگر چند is a fixed concessive phrase meaning 'although / even though' (literally 'if though'); با کینه is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'with hatred' (با = with, کینه = malice/grudge); بشتافتند is the past verb 'they rushed' (prefixed poetic form). Together the clause concedes that they had attacked in malice despite what happened.
segments.s202-g47: کنون‌گر is a classical conditional compound (کنون 'now' + گر 'if') meaning 'if now' or 'now if'; تویی is the 2nd-person present 'you are'; پهلوان سپاه is a noun phrase 'champion/hero of the army'. The whole segment sets up a conditional address: 'Now, if you are the army's champion...'.
segments.s202-g48: چنانچون is a comparative/manner word 'just as/such as'; ترا here is the object 'you' (given as the 2nd-person suffix ت + object marker را in the listing), but in sense it marks the person for whom the subsequent obligation holds; باید is a modal 'it is proper/ought', and از من بخواه is an imperative 'ask of me'. The literal flow: 'Ask of me (from me) what, just as, ought to you' → 'ask of me what is due to you.'
segments.s202-g17: ز پیش is a prepositional phrase 'from before / from the front' (ز = 'from' in poetic register + پیش = 'before/front'). "فریبرز رهام" is an appositive name sequence (Rohām associated with Fariborz — Rohām is being identified by his father/line). گرد here is a verbal/poetic element related to motion (part of the verbal complex that follows) and should be read with the following verb (i.e. it helps form the idea of turning/going out).
segments.s202-g18: برون‌رفت is the action 'went out / went forth' (a verbal noun used narratively as a past action). The conjunction و links actions. پیغام و نامه are the direct objects 'a message and a letter.' ببرد (from برد) here means 'take / carry' (the sense 'carry/take' — in epic Persian the b- prefix frequently appears in narrative verb forms). Together: 'he went out and carried message and letter.'
segments.s202-g19: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came.' طلایه 'the vanguard' is the subject following the verb (common in poetry). بدیدش is the past form 'saw him' with the enclitic object -ش ('him'). براه is a locative phrase 'on the road / en route' (ب‑ + راه). Word order is Verb — Subject — Verb+object — locative.
segments.s202-g20: بپرسیدش is the narrative past 'they asked him' (the b‑ prefix appears in many classical narrative forms; the final -ش is the object pronoun 'him'). از governs the topics asked about: 'از نام' = 'about the name.' وز here functions as a classical fused conjunction (و + ز) linking the two topics, so 'وز جایگاه' = 'and (about) the place/status.' جایگاه means 'place, station, or rank.'
segments.s202-g21: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' — in the text it's reported speech introduced by this phrase). رهام is the speaker's name. جنگی منم is a short predication: جنگی 'warrior/warlike' and منم 'I am' (منم = من + م contracted 'I am'), so 'I am a warrior.'
segments.s202-g22: This is a string of predicates joined by و 'and': هنرمند 'skillful/skillful man' (used as a predicate), بیدار 'alert/awake', سنگی 'stony / hard-hearted / hard as stone'. The final منم repeats 'I am' for emphasis (a common poetic device: predicate + منم). Each adjective is predicated of the speaker.
segments.s202-g23: پیام = 'message.' فریبرز کاوس شاه is a name cluster: فریبرز (Fariborz) qualified by کاوس شاه (Kāvus, the king) — read as 'the message of Fariborz (of King Kāvus)' (appositive/attribution identifying the person who sent the message).
segments.s202-g24: به پیران رسانم is first-person verbal phrase 'I will / I shall deliver to Pīrān' (به = to; پیران here is the addressee/recipient, the proper name Pīrān; رسانم from رساندن is 1st person singular present/subjunctive used narratively as 'I will deliver'). بدین رزمگاه is a locative phrase 'to this battlefield' (بدین = 'to this' = به + دین (classical compound), رزمگاه = 'battlefield'); together they specify where the message will be delivered.

sentences.s202-s33: He began to uncover the heads of the beauties; having set down all counsel, he began to give orders.
sentences.s202-s34: They marched forth and arrayed the army; in a short while they put everything in order.
sentences.s202-s13: When the vanguard of horsemen approached from ahead, raising dust, he remembered all the words.
sentences.s202-s14: Then Roham and Goudarz came from that battlefield toward the army's champion.
sentences.s202-s15: He commanded that they bring him before him; they brought him generous and fresh-faced.
sentences.s202-s16: Roham's poet came before him and cried, 'Beware his hidden ill‑thought.'
sentences.s202-s25: If for now you want to delay a month, no mounted troops will come from the army to the fight.
sentences.s202-s26: But if you seek battle, leave me aside; array (the troops) and draw up the battle line.
sentences.s202-s27: If for a month you entertain this hope, that you will pass beyond the frontier of Turan.
sentences.s202-s28: Drive the army toward your frontier; at once inspect all your wealth.
sentences.s202-s1: Fariborz placed a hat upon his head, for he was both a champion and a son of the king.
sentences.s202-s2: After that he ordered Roham to find Gohar-nam.
sentences.s202-s3: He told him, 'Go, stride proudly before the elders, and carry a message from me to that champion.'
sentences.s202-s4: Tell him that the turns of heaven's course have always been thus — full of pain and affection.
sentences.s202-s17: When he saw Piran, he addressed him, asked him questions, and set him upon the throne.
sentences.s202-s18: Roham brought the secret out of concealment; Faryburz spoke (the) message to him.
sentences.s202-s19: Thus Piran said, turning to Roham: 'This battle cannot be reckoned by wisdom.'
sentences.s202-s20: We have not seen you make such a bad preemptive move in battle, with Tūs's counsel and delay.
sentences.s202-s5: The turning wheel raises one up high, and makes another miserable, despised, and base.
sentences.s202-s6: There was someone whom misfortune immediately sought about.
sentences.s202-s7: Sappers/excavators do not mount night-raids; if someone is struck, it is by a heavy mace.
sentences.s202-s8: If you, with a delay, cause a pause, we will bring delay; if your counsel is for war, we will bring war.
sentences.s202-s29: Otherwise, thrust into battle — bring your grip; from now on crave neither time nor delay.
sentences.s202-s30: Someone adorned Roham with a robe of honour, just as was fitting to his name.
sentences.s202-s31: He brought the letter to Fariburz in Roham’s presence, just as he had carried it.
sentences.s202-s32: When Fariburz found a day free from delay, he sprang toward every side like a lion’s claw.
sentences.s202-s21: To the border came a huge wolf; fearless, it slew both small and great.
sentences.s202-s22: How many he killed and how many he carried off — he reckoned this border equally good for himself.
sentences.s202-s23: Now they received the retribution for this evil, though they had rushed on with hatred.
sentences.s202-s24: Now — if you are the champion of the host — ask of me what is due to you.
sentences.s202-s9: From the front Rohām (son of Fariborz) went out and carried a message and a letter.
sentences.s202-s10: The vanguard came, saw him on the road, and asked him his name and his place (origin/rank).
sentences.s202-s11: Rohām said to him, 'I am a warrior; I am skilful, alert, and hard as stone.'
sentences.s202-s12: I will deliver to Pīrān the message of Fariborz (of King Kāvus) here to this battlefield.
